channel.title: Drupal Canvas
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Canvas.

item.title: #3552914: Unify import maps between backend and code editor preview
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 88aa12c · +187/-82 prod · +69/-3 test · +19/-6 infra · 13 files
  • Discussion: #3552914 · 5 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

Canvas code components rely on JavaScript import maps to resolve module specifiers. Previously, the import map logic was duplicated between the backend and the code editor preview UI, creating maintenance burden and inconsistency risk. This change unifies import map generation on the backend, passing it to the UI via drupalSettings. A new hook allows modules and themes to add or replace import map entries, enabling custom JavaScript libraries and Tailwind CSS plugins to be made available to code components.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New hook_canvas_importmap_alter() allows modules and themes to add or modify JavaScript import map entries for Canvas code components

Technical details

The GlobalImports service now builds the complete import map structure including both global imports and scoped imports, returning an array with imports and scopes keys matching the import map specification. The service invokes hook_canvas_importmap_alter() to allow modules and themes to modify the map before cache-busting query strings are appended to global imports.

The code editor preview previously duplicated the import map in the TypeScript Preview component. It now reads existing import map tags from the main document, merges in preview-specific additions (the auto-save endpoint for component dependencies), and injects them into the iframe. This eliminates the duplication and ensures the preview uses the same import map as the backend.

The JsComponent plugin and CanvasController now call getImportMap() instead of the former getGlobalImports() method. Scoped imports (component-specific dependencies) are merged with the base map structure.

A test module demonstrates the hook by adding a new package, overriding an existing one, and adding scoped imports. The Astro bundler configuration was updated to handle Preact subpath imports correctly, ensuring bare specifiers are preserved in the output so they resolve through the import map with proper cache-busting.

Upgrade

Implement hook_canvas_importmap_alter() to add custom JavaScript libraries or Tailwind CSS plugins:

function my_module_canvas_importmap_alter(array &$import_maps): void {
  $module_path = \Drupal::service('extension.path.resolver')
    ->getPath('module', 'my_module');

  // Add a new package.
  $import_maps['imports']['my-library'] = \base_path() . $module_path . '/js/my-library.js';

  // Replace an existing package.
  $import_maps['imports']['clsx'] = \base_path() . $module_path . '/js/custom-clsx.js';

  // Add scoped imports.
  $import_maps['scopes']['/my-scope/'] = [
    'preact' => \base_path() . $module_path . '/js/custom-preact.js',
  ];
}

The hook runs for both modules and themes. Cache-busting query strings are appended automatically after the hook fires. See the new documentation at /apis/import-maps for Tailwind CSS plugin examples.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 17, 2025
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (4 months later)

Key contributors

  • wotnak (Smartbees)
  • hooroomoo (Acquia)
  • balintbrews (Acquia)
  • mglaman (Acquia)
  • larowlan (PreviousNext)
  • nagwani
  • effulgentsia (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in October 2025 and committed in March 2026 after 19 comments. The initial proposal identified duplication between backend and preview import map logic. Comment #4 from balintbrews noted a discussion with effulgentsia about using core's #import_maps render array attribute, though ultimately a Canvas-specific hook was chosen with the understanding it may be deprecated when core's hook_importmap_alter() from #3398525 becomes available. larowlan in comment #3 emphasized the importance of considering future API changes since "APIs are forever." The solution required careful coordination between the PHP service, React preview component, and Astro bundler configuration to ensure import map consistency across all contexts.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3548282: Cannot delete default image from an optional image: it reappears
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 97d9895 · +912/-29 prod · +307/-8 test · 23 files
  • Discussion: #3548282 · 7 contributors · 35 comments

Summary

Optional image and video properties in components can now be properly cleared. Previously, when users removed a default image from an optional property, it would reappear after page refresh or publish. The system now correctly distinguishes between "no value set yet" (use default) and "user intentionally removed the value" (respect empty state). This fix ensures that removing a default image from an optional property persists across page loads and publishes, preventing the default from unexpectedly returning.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The issue stemmed from the backend incorrectly falling back to DefaultRelativeUrlPropSource whenever an optional image/video prop had an empty value, even when the user explicitly removed it. The fix introduces explicit tracking of user deletion intent by preserving NULL values in prop sources.

Backend changes in GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentSourceBase now check whether the client sent an explicit value key that differs from the default. When a user removes an optional prop, the system stores it as StaticPropSource with value: null instead of falling back to the default. The validation step was updated to skip ComponentValidator checks for optional props whose value evaluated to NULL, preventing false errors. The model serialization preserves NULL values explicitly to maintain the distinction between "unset" and "deleted".

Frontend changes added a new DefaultImagePreview React component that displays a thumbnail of the default image with a "Remove default" button (only for optional props). Clicking the button sends a PATCH request with value: null to persist the deletion. The component is integrated via a custom element in the media library fieldset template and only renders when no user-selected media exists and the current value matches the default.

The fieldWidgetCompleteFormAlter hook now passes #component_prop_name through the render array to identify which prop each widget manages, enabling correct scoping when multiple image props exist in the same component form.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: September 23, 2025
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (5 months later)

Collaboration

This bug was introduced in late July 2025 by #3534601 (video prop support) and went undetected because an end-to-end test assertion fired before the unwanted default image reappeared. Git bisect quickly identified the problematic line in GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentSourceBase.php.

The fix required careful coordination between frontend and backend to distinguish user intent from system defaults. The issue links to three related past issues (#3518253, #3508170, #3528393) that created the complex state management around optional images, explaining why this regression occurred.

Multiple iterations addressed edge cases like multiple image props in one component form and ensuring required images cannot have defaults removed. The final implementation added both unit tests and Playwright tests covering SDC components, code components, and mixed required/optional scenarios.

Lessons learned

  • When implementing optional form fields with defaults, distinguish between 'no value set yet' (use default) and 'user intentionally cleared the value' (respect empty state). Store explicit deletion as a NULL value in the data layer rather than falling back to defaults, so the user's intent to leave the field empty is preserved across saves. (via bnjmnm, narendrar, wim leers, tedbow)
  • When async operations complete after assertions run, tests may pass while the system enters an incorrect state. Add delays or wait conditions that ensure the full state transition completes before asserting the final outcome. (via bnjmnm)
  • When multiple instances of the same widget exist in a form, ensure state changes target only the intended instance. Pass unique identifiers through render arrays to prevent cross-contamination when JavaScript manipulates widget state. (via bnjmnm)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3554197: Content templates: support linking multiple-cardinality field widgets
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ad6e9fd · +184/-1 prod · +1/-1 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3554197 · 5 contributors · 36 comments

Summary

Canvas now supports linking multiple-value fields (like a tags field that can store several taxonomy terms) to component properties. Previously, the linker button did not appear for fields that allow multiple values because they render their label differently than single-value fields. This feature enables content editors to connect multi-value entity fields to components that accept arrays, making it possible to display lists of tags, categories, or other multi-value content in custom components.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Multi-value field widgets in Drupal render their labels as an <h4> element inside a table header, unlike single-value fields that use standard #title_display rendering. The existing linker logic only attached to elements with #title_display, so it never appeared for multi-value fields.

The fix adds logic in canvas_stark_preprocess_field_multiple_value_form() to detect when #prop_link_data is available on a multiple-value field element. When present, it reconstructs the table header to wrap both the existing <h4> and a new linker element in a container with flexbox styling.

A key dependency is #3550848, which added a titleDisplay !== 'invisible' check in DrupalFormElementLabel.tsx. This prevents duplicate linkers from appearing on individual rows within multi-value fields. Drupal core sets #title_display => 'invisible' for individual items in multi-value fields (see WidgetBase.php lines 207-213), so the linker only renders once in the table header where titleDisplay is not invisible.

The solution is theme-layer only because the React component already handles the titleDisplay filtering correctly.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 24, 2025
  • Committed: March 11, 2026 (4 months later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)
  • narendrar (Acquia)
  • chandu7929
  • tedbow (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue took four months from opening to commit, with significant complexity around finding the right approach. An earlier attempt made changes to GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentSourceBase.php to prevent duplicate linkers on each row of multi-value fields. However, during development, #3550848 was committed with a more general solution that made those changes unnecessary.

The git history was accidentally lost through force-pushing, causing confusion during review. tedbow investigated why the earlier approach was no longer needed and documented the analysis in #17, confirming that the titleDisplay !== 'invisible' check from #3550848 already solved the duplicate linker problem. This allowed the final solution to be simpler, requiring only theme-layer changes.

The issue also benefited from #3546760 landing during development, which added end-to-end template tests. This enabled proper test coverage to be added, including verification that both values from a multi-value field appear in the component preview.

Lessons learned

  • When rendering UI controls for form elements, check how multi-value widgets structure their markup. Multi-value field widgets in Drupal render labels as table headers (e.g., <h4> inside <th>) rather than using standard #title_display properties, so controls attached via title processing hooks won't appear. Handle multi-value cases explicitly in preprocessing hooks like hook_preprocess_field_multiple_value_form(). (via bnjmnm)
  • When propagating metadata to child form elements, check if child elements intentionally hide their labels with #title_display => 'invisible'. In multi-value fields, Drupal core sets individual items to invisible because the parent widget renders a shared label. Respect this by filtering rendering based on title_display to avoid duplicate UI controls on every child element. (via tedbow)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3570560: PHPCS: in_array() → \in_array()
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a09cef8 · +111/-111 prod · +16/-16 test · +2/-2 infra · 74 files
  • Discussion: #3570560 · 2 contributors · 21 comments

Summary

Canvas now requires the in_array() function to be written as \in_array() throughout the codebase. This is a code style change that makes PHP slightly faster by avoiding namespace lookups for this commonly used function. The change applies only to Canvas's internal code and does not affect how site builders or module developers write their own code.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change adds in_array to the list of global functions that must be fully qualified with a leading backslash in Canvas's phpcs.xml configuration. The SlevomatCodingStandard.Namespaces.FullyQualifiedGlobalFunctions rule now enforces \in_array() instead of in_array().

The leading backslash tells PHP to skip namespace resolution and call the global function directly. This provides a small performance benefit in namespaced code by eliminating unnecessary symbol table lookups. The change affects 180+ call sites across the codebase.

The diff shows the mechanical replacement of in_array( with \in_array( and !in_array( with !\in_array(. The updated phpcs.xml now includes a reference to PHP's internal compile-time optimization list, documenting why certain functions receive this treatment.

This is part of a gradual rollout to eventually apply this standard to all global function calls in Canvas, building on earlier work with assert(), sprintf(), array_map(), and type-checking functions like is_array().

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 30, 2026
  • Committed: March 10, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • gurnoor kaur

Collaboration

This task was part of a larger effort to enforce fully qualified global function calls in Canvas's codebase, following #3567950. The issue sat for a month before a contributor volunteered. After an initial merge request encountered test failures and PHPStan violations unrelated to the mechanical change, the maintainer stepped in to fix the issues that had emerged from incorrect find-and-replace patterns (!in_array( → \in_array( instead of !\in_array(). The maintainer noted this consumed extra review time and emphasized the importance of using the precise search-and-replace technique documented in the issue summary.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3578094: Fix REST API violation of the Canvas Page PATCH operation: auto-save operations should be auto-save prefixed
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ec9cbe3 · +190/-150 prod · +12/-12 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3578094 · 2 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

The Canvas module's publish and unpublish API endpoint violated REST principles and lacked consistency with other auto-save operations. The endpoint operated on auto-save storage but was located at a path intended for the external Canvas Page API. This created confusion about which operations were stateful versus stateless and blocked work on the proper external API.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The PATCH operation for publishing and unpublishing Canvas pages accepted a clientInstanceId parameter and saved to auto-save storage, making it stateful. However, it used the path /canvas/api/v0/content/canvas_page/{canvas_page}, which was needed for a truly stateless external Canvas Page API being developed in #3575144.

The fix moved the entire PATCH operation from ApiContentControllers::patch() to a new ApiContentAutoSaveControllers::patch() controller and changed the route path to /canvas/api/v0/content/auto-save/canvas_page/{canvas_page}. This aligns with other auto-save endpoints like canvas.api.config.auto-save.patch which already used the auto-save path prefix.

The change also extracted the homepage checking logic from ApiContentControllers into a new HomePageHelper service, improving code organization and allowing the logic to be shared between controllers. The frontend JavaScript and all link generation logic were updated to use the new endpoint path. OpenAPI documentation was reorganized to reflect that this endpoint manages auto-save storage, not direct entity updates.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 9, 2026
  • Committed: March 10, 2026

Key contributors

  • penyaskito (Acquia)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • tedbow (Acquia)
  • mglaman (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was opened and resolved quickly in just two days. Penyaskito identified the REST API violation during Canvas development and provided a complete solution in the issue summary. Longwave provided detailed code review feedback about using a service versus a trait for the homepage helper logic. After addressing the review feedback and confirming the Playwright test failures were unrelated flakiness rather than legitimate regressions, the fix was approved and committed. The rapid turnaround kept #3575144 unblocked.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577767: Linker not appearing for `type: string, format: date`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6a1adff · +51/-7 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3577767 · 2 contributors · 10 comments

Summary

The linker feature was not working for date-only fields in component templates. When site builders tried to connect a date field to dynamic data, the linker button would not appear. This prevented date fields from being linked to content properties like "Authored on" or "Changed" dates. The fix ensures that date fields show the linker button just like other field types, allowing site builders to make date fields dynamic in their component templates.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The linker appears for most field widgets automatically, but some widgets use custom wrapper templates instead of the standard form element label template. Previously, only fieldsets received the prop link data needed to render the linker. Date-only fields use datetime_wrapper as their theme wrapper, which was not included in this logic.

The fix adds datetime_wrapper to the list of wrappers that receive prop link data in GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentSourceBase. The corresponding theme preprocess function and Twig template were updated to render the linker using the same canvas-drupal-label component already used by fieldsets.

This mirrors the approach from #3550848 which fixed linker rendering for date-time fields. The test suite was expanded to verify that date-only fields can be linked to various date properties and that the linked values display correctly in component previews.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 7, 2026
  • Committed: March 9, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was fixed within two days of being reported. Wim Leers initially deferred to bnjmnm given the Redux-integrated field widget context, and flagged #3550848 as closely related. The solution followed the same pattern established in that earlier issue, extending the wrapper detection logic to include datetime_wrapper alongside fieldset. The fix included comprehensive test coverage to prevent regression.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3558448: UI must only set `value` on `StaticPropSource`s, not on `DynamicPropSource`s nor `HostEntityUrlPropSource`s
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ecbce82 · +56/-85 · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3558448 · 3 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

The Canvas UI was incorrectly sending a value field when linking dynamic or host entity URL data sources to component properties. This caused the server to receive unnecessary data and in some cases triggered errors. The UI was originally built around static prop sources, and dynamic sources were added later without fully refactoring the linking mechanism. This fix updates the prop linker to only send the source reference, not a value placeholder, making the data flow cleaner and preventing the server from receiving malformed requests.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The PropLinker component was sending {[propName]: 'noop'} in the values object whenever linking any prop source type, even though only StaticPropSource needs a value field. DynamicPropSource and HostEntityUrlPropSource only require a source reference since their values are computed server-side.

The fix introduces a new action _linkPropToEntityValue that replaces _updateExistingComponentValuesForLinking specifically for prop linking scenarios. This new action only updates the source field in the model and explicitly removes any resolved value for the linked prop by deleting it from the resolved object before sending to the server.

The refactored code checks that a valid evaluated component model exists and that the prop exists in the component metadata before proceeding. It then sends only the updated source and cleaned resolved object to the updateComponent endpoint, eliminating the unnecessary value field that was causing issues.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 18, 2025
  • Committed: March 9, 2026 (3 months later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • jessebaker (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue emerged from work on #3555068 where a workaround was added server-side to handle the incorrect data from the UI. The team recognized this was technical debt from the original static-prop-centric UI implementation, with dynamic sources bolted on later under tight deadlines.

Jesse Baker attempted an initial fix but got stuck understanding the interaction between client and server. He also discovered a related bug during testing which was split into #3558719. Wim Leers proposed a simpler approach: don't send a value field at all for non-static sources. The team agreed to pair program to bridge frontend and backend knowledge gaps.

Benjamin Melançon ultimately implemented the solution, creating a dedicated action for prop linking that cleanly separates concerns. The fix merged after confirming it simplified the programmatic linking code while fixing the core issue.

Lessons learned

  • When sending data to an API that only accepts application/x-www-form-urlencoded, JavaScript undefined values are serialized as the string literal "undefined" rather than being omitted. Either omit the key-value pair entirely from the object before serialization, or switch to application/json where undefined values are automatically omitted during JSON.stringify(). (via jessebaker)
  • When building UI features around new abstractions (like DynamicPropSource) on top of a system designed for older patterns (like StaticPropSource), bolting on new functionality without refactoring creates technical debt. Under aggressive deadlines, this is understandable and necessary, but the underlying assumptions should be refactored once time permits. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3556265: Create ability to unpublish Canvas pages
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 2418312 · +1265/-146 prod · +657/-25 test · 32 files
  • Discussion: #3556265 · 16 contributors · 95 comments

Summary

Canvas Pages can now be unpublished after being published. Previously, there was no way to take a published page offline while keeping it editable. This feature lets content editors hide pages from visitors by marking them as unpublished through the Canvas editor. The unpublish action is staged through the same auto-save and "Review changes" workflow that Canvas uses for other edits. Draft pages that have never been published work differently from unpublished pages, and the interface shows the correct status for each. The homepage cannot be unpublished to prevent breaking the site. This feature is built to avoid conflicts with future Workspaces integration planned for later in 2026.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas Pages now support an "Unpublish" action that allows published pages to be taken offline. This is implemented through a new PATCH endpoint (canvas.api.content.patch) that updates the auto-save entry with the new published status. When a user clicks "Unpublish page" in the editor, it stages the status change in auto-save rather than immediately updating the live entity. The change takes effect when the user publishes through the "Review changes" workflow, consistent with how other Canvas edits work.

The implementation distinguishes between three states without adding a new field: published pages (status = 1), unpublished pages (status = 0 and previously published), and draft pages (status = 0 and never published). Draft detection relies on the existing AutoSaveManager::entityIsConsideredNew() logic, which checks if the page title is still the default "Untitled page." This avoids adding database fields that would complicate the planned migration to Workspaces.

The PATCH endpoint validates that only the status field is being updated and prevents unpublishing the homepage by checking both current and staged homepage configuration. Auto-save cache tags ensure the UI updates immediately when status changes are staged. The navigation UI shows badges for unpublished pages and pages pending unpublish, with different indicators for saved versus unsaved status changes.

The implementation avoids Views plugins initially proposed in earlier merge requests, keeping the solution simpler and reducing technical debt for the Workspaces migration.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 5, 2025
  • Committed: March 9, 2026 (4 months later)

Lessons learned

  • When caching happens at multiple layers, invalidating an inner component doesn't help if an outer cache still serves stale data. Avoid adding fields that represent derived state when that state can be computed from existing data, especially if the derived field will become obsolete when migrating to a different subsystem. Computing state dynamically (like distinguishing draft from archived by checking revision history) avoids data migration complexity and conflicting systems during transitions. (via tedbow, lauriii)
  • When building a temporary solution before a planned migration to a different subsystem, minimize changes that will require deprecation and removal. Evaluate whether existing contributed modules solve the use case adequately, even if imperfectly, rather than building custom implementations that create technical debt during the migration period. (via catch, amateescu, tedbow)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577627: Add support for TypeScript syntax in @drupal-canvas/eslint-config
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 9b8176c · +6/-0 prod · +16/-0 test · +10/-22 infra · 7 files
  • Discussion: #3577627 · 3 contributors · 9 comments

Summary

The @drupal-canvas/eslint-config package can now properly parse and lint TypeScript syntax in Canvas components. Previously, the package could lint TypeScript files but lacked the parser needed to understand TypeScript-specific syntax like interfaces and type annotations. This fix ensures TypeScript components are validated correctly during development, catching errors that would otherwise slip through.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The @drupal-canvas/eslint-config package added typescript-eslint as a dependency and integrated it into all configuration variants. The required config now uses tseslint.parser as its language parser, enabling ESLint to understand TypeScript syntax like interfaces, type annotations, and generics.

The recommended config includes tseslint.configs.recommended rules, while the strict config adds tseslint.configs.strict for more rigorous TypeScript validation. These changes build on top of the existing React and YAML linting rules.

The fix addresses an issue introduced in #3577508, where TypeScript file extensions were added to the linting file patterns but the parser was not configured to handle TypeScript syntax. The package-lock.json changes remove the "dev": true flag from typescript-eslint and its dependencies, making them production dependencies rather than dev-only packages. Test coverage was expanded to include a TypeScript component example with interface declarations.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026

Key contributors

  • wotnak (Smartbees)
  • hooroomoo (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was opened and fixed on the same day (2026-03-06). wotnak identified the bug shortly after the previous issue #3577508 added TypeScript file support. hooroomoo and balintbrews both committed to the issue fork, with the solution quickly reaching completion. The fast turnaround suggests clear understanding of the problem and straightforward implementation.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568264: Update code component props schema to support 'array' type
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 789acc7 · +352/-70 prod · +619/-71 test · +5/-1 infra · 18 files
  • Discussion: #3568264 · 3 contributors · 22 comments

Summary

Code components created through the Canvas UI can now define array-type properties, enabling lists of strings, numbers, booleans, or objects (like multiple images). Previously, only single-value properties were supported. This allows ambitious site builders to create more flexible components without writing PHP code, such as a card carousel with multiple images or a tag list with multiple labels. Array properties support optional maximum item limits and work with all existing property types that Canvas already supports.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas code components (JavaScriptComponent config entities) store their metadata in YAML, including prop definitions that follow JSON Schema conventions. This issue added support for type: array with an items schema defining the type of each array element. The implementation validates both single-cardinality props (matching the 5 basic JSON Schema types: boolean, integer, number, string, object) and array-cardinality props (sequences of those types).

The config schema definition uses Drupal's dynamic typing system to validate prop examples based on both the prop's type and its items.type. This required leveraging TypedConfigManager::getDefinitionWithReplacements() with replacement tokens like [%parent.%parent.type] and [%parent.%parent.items.type] to create 10 distinct example validation schemas (5 basic types × 2 cardinalities).

The JsonSchemaObject class was extended to find $ref values for both single-object props and array-of-objects props by traversing the parent chain differently for each case. A maxItems constraint validates minimum value of 2 (enforcing arrays are actually multi-value). The implementation also worked around a Drupal core cache pollution bug (#3400181) with a temporary TypedConfigManagerWithCachePollutionFix class that prevents caching dynamically-typed definitions.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 19, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • isholgueras (Acquia)
  • tedbow (Acquia)
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • tim.plunkett (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue took about 6 weeks from opening to commit, with 22 comments. The initial implementation by isholgueras and tedbow encountered complexity around config schema validation for array types. The breakthrough came when wim leers joined to tackle the dynamic typing challenge, referencing a similar issue from exactly a year prior. He discovered TypedConfigManager::getDefinitionWithReplacements() could handle the complexity elegantly, eliminating a workaround for a different core bug. However, this trade revealed a cache pollution bug in core (#3400181), requiring a temporary fix in Canvas. Leers worked late to ensure E2E tests passed, acknowledging the issue was "hard-blocking" the team. The collaboration highlighted Canvas's role as a proving ground for advanced config schema patterns that will benefit Drupal core.

Lessons learned

  • When config schema needs to support dynamic types with variable parameters, use TypedConfigManager::getDefinitionWithReplacements() to expand types with replacements rather than building custom workarounds. This leverages Drupal's built-in mechanism for handling dynamic non-top-level config schema types and reduces complexity. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577508: Update eslint rules for the more flexible component discovery
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: cc51b16 · +79/-46 prod · +118/-37 test · +17/-8 infra · 19 files
  • Discussion: #3577508 · 2 contributors · 7 comments

Summary

Drupal Canvas now supports more flexible component metadata files. Components can now use either the traditional index-style metadata (component.yml in a component directory) or the new named-style metadata (e.g., button.component.yml alongside other components in a shared directory). The validation rules have been updated to accommodate both styles while maintaining consistency checks. The older, stricter validation rules remain available for the deprecated build-d command to ensure backward compatibility.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change updates the @drupal-canvas/eslint-config package to support the new flexible component discovery introduced in #3571534. The original required ESLint config has been renamed to requiredDeprecated and is now used exclusively by the deprecated npx canvas build-d command.

A new required config was created with relaxed rules. Three strict rules were removed from the new config and marked as deprecated: component-files, component-imports, and component-no-hierarchy. These rules enforced the old rigid structure where each component lived in its own directory with specific file naming requirements.

The remaining rules were updated to support both index-style metadata (component.yml in a dedicated component directory) and named-style metadata (e.g., button.component.yml in a shared directory). The component-dir-name rule now validates that the machineName matches either the directory name (index-style) or the filename prefix before .component.yml (named-style). The component-exports rule was refactored to use a new isComponentEntrypoint() utility that correctly identifies entry files for both styles.

TypeScript file extensions (.ts, .tsx) were added to the file patterns across all config variants, expanding beyond the original JavaScript-only support.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026

Key contributors

  • wotnak (Smartbees)
  • hooroomoo (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was opened and resolved on the same day (2026-03-06), with only two active contributors. After wotnak created the issue, hooroomoo made their first commit to the fork and delivered the complete implementation. The quick turnaround and minimal discussion suggest the scope was well-defined from the parent issue #3571534, allowing for straightforward execution. This appears to be a follow-up task that was cleanly separated from the larger component discovery work.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576865: Allow adding custom fonts via AssetLibrary
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 08eb55f · +42/-0 prod · +137/-0 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3576865 · 1 contributor · 8 comments

Summary

AssetLibrary entities can now track custom font files. This feature allows fonts to be added programmatically through the API, with font CSS files included in the generated asset library. This is primarily for the CLI code component push commands, though the editor could expose this in the UI later. The fonts are stored as a list of name and URI pairs, and the CSS files are automatically added to the library when rendered.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

AssetLibrary entities gain a new fonts property to track custom font CSS files. The property stores a nullable array of entries, each with a name and uri field pointing to a managed file.

The AssetLibrary entity now includes getFonts() and setFonts() methods. When fonts are set, they are included in the client-side normalization and persisted to configuration. The default value is null, which getFonts() normalizes to an empty array.

In LibraryHooks::libraryInfoBuild(), font entries are processed using the FileUrlGeneratorInterface to generate public URLs from the managed file URIs. These URLs are added to the library definition as theme CSS assets, ensuring they load when the asset library attaches to a page.

Configuration schema defines fonts as a nullable sequence of mappings with name and uri string properties. The global asset library configuration includes the new fonts: null default. Comprehensive test coverage validates saving, loading, clearing, client-side normalization, and library discovery integration.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 3, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (2 days later)

Key contributors

  • mglaman (Acquia)
  • attilatilman (Acquia)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577399: Detect legacy CANVAS_COMPONENT_DIR and migrate to canvas.config.json
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: d44b9d8 · +118/-0 prod · +87/-0 test · +4/-0 infra · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3577399 · 1 contributor · 5 comments

Summary

The Canvas CLI now helps projects migrate from the deprecated CANVAS_COMPONENT_DIR environment variable to the new canvas.config.json configuration file. When the CLI detects that a project is still using the legacy environment variable, it displays a warning and offers to automatically create or update the configuration file with the componentDir setting. This prevents existing projects from breaking after the configuration approach changed. In non-interactive mode, the CLI provides clear manual migration instructions instead of prompting.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The CLI now runs a migration check before executing any command through a preAction hook. The handleLegacyComponentDirMigration() function detects when CANVAS_COMPONENT_DIR is set in the environment but componentDir is not defined in canvas.config.json. It warns the user that the environment variable is deprecated.

For the current CLI run, it preserves backward compatibility by setting both componentDir and deprecatedComponentDir in the runtime config. This ensures commands continue to work while migration is in progress.

In interactive mode, the function prompts the user to create or update canvas.config.json with the componentDir value from the environment variable. If the user confirms, it writes the configuration file, either creating a new one or merging the setting into an existing file. If the user declines or cancels, it shows a message explaining how to migrate manually later.

In non-interactive mode (when the --yes flag is passed), the function skips prompts entirely and displays manual migration instructions. A guard flag prevents the migration logic from running multiple times in the same process. Tests can reset this flag using resetLegacyComponentDirMigrationForTests().

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 5, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026

Key contributors

  • balintbrews (Acquia)
  • hooroomoo (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was opened and resolved on the same day with a single commit. The fast turnaround suggests this was identified as an important backward compatibility concern during a larger refactoring to move configuration from environment variables to canvas.config.json. The issue includes a screenshot showing the warning and prompt flow, indicating careful attention to user experience during the migration path.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577343: Bump eslint-config package to 0.1.2 and update CLI to it
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: fdf3ae1 · +1/-1 prod · +164/-183 infra · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3577343 · 2 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

This update bumps the @drupal-canvas/eslint-config package to version 0.1.2 and updates the CLI dependency to use this new version. The change includes several dependency updates in the package lockfile and a minor placeholder text adjustment in the scaffold command. This ensures the CLI uses the latest ESLint configuration and keeps dependencies current with no security vulnerabilities.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change addresses three tasks: updating the @drupal-canvas/eslint-config package to version 0.1.2, updating the CLI's dependency reference to match, and running npm audit to verify no vulnerabilities exist.

The diff shows the version bump in packages/eslint-config/package.json from 0.1.1 to 0.1.2, with the CLI's package.json updating its dependency from ^0.1.0 to ^0.1.2. The package lockfile reflects numerous dependency updates across the ecosystem, including updates to packages like @hono/node-server, @microlink/react-json-view, dompurify, express-rate-limit, and others. New Rolldown bindings for Linux ppc64 and s390x architectures were added as optional dependencies.

A minor user-facing change updates the scaffold command's placeholder text from 'my-component' to 'hello-world' to better match the machine name format expected by the template. This is a cosmetic improvement for developer experience when scaffolding new components.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 5, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3555062: Simplify canvas_stark: remove semi-coupled theme engine + improve React embedding
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b341f14 · +833/-661 prod · +27/-19 test · +101/-220 infra · 90 files
  • Discussion: #3555062 · 4 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

The Canvas Stark theme now uses custom HTML elements in Twig templates instead of a separate theme engine to embed React components. This simplifies how forms render in the Canvas UI by making templates more readable and reducing the number of internal processing stages. Instead of JSON configuration files that few people understood, developers now work with familiar Twig syntax that includes custom tags like <drupal-canvas-form> which map directly to React components. The change removes approximately 500 lines of theme engine code while achieving the same functionality.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas previously used a custom "semi-coupled" theme engine that processed Twig templates containing JSON configuration. These JSON files mapped render array properties to React component props, requiring developers to understand both the theme engine's special syntax and its processing pipeline.

The new approach eliminates the theme engine entirely. Twig templates now use custom HTML elements (like <drupal-canvas-input> or <drupal-canvas-textarea>) that Hyperscriptify converts to React components on the client side. A new CanvasPropVisitor Twig node visitor detects when variables appear inside these custom element attributes and automatically wraps them with a jsx_attributes filter. This filter converts Attribute objects and arrays to JSON, ensuring React receives properly formatted props.

For props that need to be elements (not strings), a new slot() Twig function wraps content in <element-prop slot="name"> tags. Hyperscriptify maps these slots to React props with element values, leveraging the Web Components slots API pattern.

The visitor includes logic to detect the children attribute within custom elements and throws a syntax error, since children is reserved in React. It parses the Twig buffer character-by-character to determine context, handling escaped quotes and tracking whether the parser is inside an attribute value.

Templates moved from process_as_jsx/ directories to regular locations. The media library still uses standard Twig templates (not React) because dialogs need rendered HTML before insertion into the page. Theme suggestion hooks moved from a dedicated SemiCoupledThemeEngineHooks class into canvas_stark.theme.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 29, 2025
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (4 months later)

Key contributors

  • bnjmnm (Acquia)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • larowlan (PreviousNext)
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue took about 4 months from opening to commit, with significant collaboration across 4 contributors and 19 comments. The approach was strongly endorsed early on by phenaproxima, who highlighted maintainability gains from removing the "dark magic" of the semi-coupled theme engine. wim leers requested clarifications on the complex Twig parsing logic but ultimately approved. phenaproxima again emphasized the clean variable naming and helpful comments made the low-level string parsing approachable despite its complexity. bnjmnm acknowledged phenaproxima for helping push the change across the finish line. A follow-up issue #3577643 was opened for adding kernel and unit tests for the new Twig enhancements.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3573096: Add Multi-Value Text Prop Support (UI)
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ac890f2 · +770/-4 prod · +463/-0 test · 14 files
  • Discussion: #3573096 · 6 contributors · 33 comments

Summary

Multi-value text fields in Canvas now display with a more compact, modern interface when the development module is enabled. Instead of showing all input boxes at once, the field shows a list of existing values. Clicking any value opens a popover where you can edit the text, and you can reorder items by dragging them. An "Add new" button appears at the bottom when you can add more values. This makes forms with many values easier to scan and work with, especially on smaller screens or when managing long lists of items.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This feature adds a popover-based editing interface for multi-value text fields, available behind the canvas_dev_mode flag. The new UI replaces the traditional stacked textfield approach with a collapsed list view where each value appears as a clickable row with a drag handle.

The implementation uses a new DrupalInputMultivalueForm React component that renders list items in a compact format. Clicking a row opens a Radix UI popover containing the actual input field. The component maintains a hidden real input for form submission while presenting a visual duplicate in the popover for editing. Changes commit when the user presses Enter, and closing the popover without Enter reverts any uncommitted edits.

The backend marks multi-value form elements via hook_field_widget_complete_form_alter() and routes them to a custom Twig template. A new input--multivalue-form.html.twig template suggestion connects these elements to the React component. CSS overrides hide default Drupal tabledrag weight controls while preserving AJAX functionality. The remove button in the popover triggers the hidden Drupal remove button to ensure proper AJAX updates.

The feature includes custom drag handle SVG icons via Drupal.theme.tableDragHandle() override and comprehensive Cypress tests covering editing, adding, removing, and reordering items. The design respects cardinality limits and shows/hides the Add button accordingly.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 12, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (20 days later)

Key contributors

  • vipin.mittal18 (Acquia)
  • utkarsh_33 (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)
  • narendrar (Acquia)
  • kunal.sachdev (Acquia)
  • chandu7929 (Acquia)

Collaboration

The issue progressed through several review rounds over three weeks, from Feb 12 to Mar 5, 2026. The initial implementation required multiple iterations to address design fidelity, accessibility concerns, and interaction patterns. bnjmnm conducted thorough code owner reviews, identifying issues with focus management, keyboard navigation, and integration with existing multivalue field patterns. The team coordinated closely with parallel work on #3546869 for data persistence, clearly delineating that this issue focused purely on UI representation while storage would be handled separately. utkarsh_33 demonstrated persistence through multiple rounds of feedback, addressing complex requirements around popover behavior, AJAX integration, and visual polish. The collaboration revealed the value of comprehensive e2e testing for complex UI interactions, with new tests written specifically for the feature-flagged design.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571534: New CLI discovery and build for Code Components
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 768de13 · +1993/-137 prod · +906/-8 test · +190/-19 infra · 51 files
  • Discussion: #3571534 · 4 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

Canvas CLI now discovers and builds Code Components automatically from any directory structure. The new build command finds components by scanning for component metadata files, bundles third-party dependencies and local imports with Vite, compiles JavaScript and CSS, and generates a manifest tracking all assets. This replaces the manual per-component workflow with automatic discovery and optimized production builds. The old build command remains available as build-d but is deprecated.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The new build command implements automatic component discovery using the @drupal-canvas/discovery package. It scans for component.yml or [name].component.yml files and matches them with JavaScript and CSS entry files following naming conventions (index or named patterns).

Build workflow: 1) Discovery scans the configured directory and validates components. 2) Import analysis walks the dependency graph from component entry files, categorizing imports as third-party packages, alias imports (@/ prefix), or relative imports. 3) Vendor bundling uses Vite to create optimized bundles for third-party packages in dist/vendor/ with code splitting and tree-shaking. Packages already provided by Drupal Canvas (React, Preact, etc.) are marked as external. 4) Local import bundling processes @/ alias imports—JavaScript modules are bundled, CSS files are copied, and image/SVG/font assets are copied preserving extensions. 5) Individual components are compiled with SWC (now supporting TypeScript/TSX syntax). 6) Tailwind CSS is generated by extracting class name candidates from all components. 7) A manifest file maps import specifiers to their bundled output paths.

Configuration uses two files: canvas.config.json (committed, defines codebase structure like componentDir, aliasBaseDir, outputDir) and .env (environmental secrets like OAuth credentials). The Vite build configuration uses the new @drupal-canvas/vite-compat plugin that provides alias resolution, SVGR for SVG imports, and handles side-effect CSS imports.

The deprecated build-d command preserves the old workflow where components are selected individually and built without Vite bundling.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 4, 2026
  • Committed: March 4, 2026 (27 days later)

Key contributors

  • balintbrews (Acquia)
  • hooroomoo (Acquia)
  • mglaman (Acquia)
  • rikki_iki
  • wotnak (Smartbees)

Collaboration

The issue evolved significantly from prototype to committed implementation over one month. balintbrews opened with a detailed spec and proof-of-concept, then extracted shared discovery and Vite compatibility packages while building Canvas Workbench in parallel (#3573867). hooroomoo implemented the build command, followed by design discussions between hooroomoo, mglaman, and balintbrews about manifest structure. They decided to drop component tracking from the manifest (comments #8-12) since components can be discovered dynamically and the manifest should focus on vendor and local assets. Testing revealed edge cases around CSS subpath imports and same-named files in different directories, which shaped the final import resolution logic.

Lessons learned

  • When building a manifest for a compilation process, track only the artifacts that cannot be rediscovered through convention. If components can be found by scanning for metadata files (e.g., .yml), exclude them from the manifest and track only vendor dependencies and imported assets that lack discoverability conventions. *(via mglaman, hooroomoo, balintbrews)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3546760: Add e2e test for `ContentTemplates` feature
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 03f9abc · +1716/-15 prod · +1/-0 infra · 50 files
  • Discussion: #3546760 · 5 contributors · 23 comments

Summary

Canvas now has comprehensive end-to-end tests covering the content template linking workflow. These tests verify that components added to templates can link their props to entity fields, and that those links work correctly when content is rendered. The tests cover a wide range of field types and nested field paths, including entity references and hierarchical data structures. This testing infrastructure ensures the template system continues working as expected as new features are added.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue added Playwright end-to-end tests for the Content Templates feature, focusing on field linking. The test suite verifies linking component props to entity fields across multiple data types (boolean, string, email, URI, integer, HTML, image objects) and field widget configurations.

A test recipe provides field setup on the Page content type along with sample content. The main test file systematically verifies each prop type in the all-props component can be linked to appropriate fields, checking both the UI state and rendered preview output. The tests validate hierarchical field paths (e.g., "Image Media > Media > Image > Height") and ensure suggestion menus work correctly.

Performance improvements were made to PropSourceSuggester to avoid repeatedly fetching suggestions in loops. The LinkedFieldBox component now displays hierarchical paths with arrows (→) in tooltips and only the final segment as the visible title. The PropLinker component was enhanced with test-friendly data attributes to enable automated testing of nested suggestion menus. The Playwright configuration increased the global timeout to one hour to accommodate the comprehensive test suite.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: September 15, 2025
  • Committed: March 4, 2026 (5 months later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm
  • jessebaker (Acquia)

Lessons learned

  • When end-to-end tests call an API repeatedly in a loop, fetch shared data once before the loop rather than on each iteration. Tests that stress performance edge cases (like components with many props) will expose inefficiencies that would otherwise remain hidden in typical use cases. (via bnjmnm)
  • When writing tests for systems with extensible options (like plugin systems or configurable widgets), design tests to fail when new options are added but not covered by tests. This prevents silent test decay as the system evolves. (via bnjmnm)
  • When planning major refactors to UI code, ensure end-to-end test coverage is in place first. Without automated tests for areas not covered by other test layers, changes that break large parts of the UI may go undetected. (via jessebaker)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576578: Inform SDC/code component developers of nonsensical `type: array` prop restrictions: `maxItems <2`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6d77b0b · +46/-1 prod · +4/-1 test · 7 files
  • Discussion: #3576578 · 4 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

Canvas now provides clearer error messages when component developers misconfigure array props. Previously, when a component schema defined an array with maxItems set to less than 2, developers received a vague "Nonsensical array size limit specified" error. The new error message explains the specific problem, shows the actual value, and suggests using a non-array type for single-value props. This helps developers understand why arrays must allow at least two items to be useful in Canvas.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

When Canvas added support for type: array props in a previous issue, it included a generic exception for the edge case where maxItems < 2. This made little sense for Canvas because a single-item array is just a pointless wrapper for a component prop, and zero or negative values make even less sense.

This issue improved developer experience by replacing the vague error message with a helpful one. The new message appears in two places: ComponentMetadataRequirementsChecker (which validates component metadata) and JsonSchemaType::computeStorablePropShape() (which interprets JSON schema for storage).

The enhanced message now shows the actual maxItems value received and explicitly suggests using a non-array type for single-value props. A test component called image-gallery-nonsensical was added with maxItems: 1 and a photo of a judging dog to verify the validation works correctly. This pays off technical debt from the initial array support implementation that intentionally left this validation incomplete.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 2, 2026
  • Committed: March 3, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • isholgueras (Acquia)
  • velmir_taky
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • tedbow (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue resolved quickly, committed within one day of being opened. It was a planned follow-up from a previous array support feature where incomplete validation was intentionally left as technical debt. The maintainer noted this was about "paying off technical debt we accepted to go faster in the summer of last year."

Multiple contributors iterated on the error message wording. The first attempt improved the message, then tedbow refined it further to be more specific. The final version includes the actual maxItems value and actionable guidance. The test component includes a humorous touch with a photo named "gracie-judging.jpg" showing a judgmental dog, which the maintainer appreciated with "😂👏 'judging Gracie'".

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572881: It's not possible to scroll the Canvas diagonally with touch pad
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.1.0
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ba84193 · +129/-43 prod · +20/-11 test · +24/-0 infra · 5 files
  • Discussion: #3572881 · 3 contributors · 7 comments

Summary

The canvas editor now supports smooth diagonal scrolling with trackpad gestures, matching the natural feel of design tools like Figma. Previously, panning with a trackpad was restricted to horizontal or vertical movements only. The improved gesture handling also provides more natural, continuous zoom behavior during pinch and scroll-to-zoom interactions, replacing the previous stepped zoom levels with fluid scaling based on gesture speed and magnitude.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The editor frame now uses the @use-gesture/react library to handle trackpad and touch gestures more naturally. The previous implementation listened to raw wheel events and processed them in discrete steps, throttling events to 50ms intervals and snapping to predefined zoom levels. This caused jerky diagonal scrolling because horizontal and vertical movements were processed separately.

The new implementation uses useGesture with separate handlers for wheel and pinch events. Free-form panning applies both X and Y deltas simultaneously, enabling true diagonal scrolling. Zoom behavior differs by browser: Chrome and Firefox fire wheel events with ctrlKey=true during trackpad pinch, which the wheel handler processes with continuous scaling based on delta magnitude. Safari fires native GestureEvents, which the pinch handler processes by writing transforms directly to the DOM during the gesture for smooth 60fps updates, then syncing to Redux when complete.

The zoom sensitivity is tuned differently for mouse wheel versus trackpad pinch gestures. Scaling is now logarithmic (proportional to current scale) rather than stepped, providing natural zoom behavior at all levels. The implementation carefully coordinates between gesture state refs and Redux to avoid conflicts between browser-specific event paths.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 11, 2026
  • Committed: March 3, 2026 (19 days later)

Key contributors

  • jessebaker (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue progressed efficiently from report to fix within three weeks. jessebaker identified the UX gap by comparing to industry-standard design tools. The proposed resolution suggested exploring gesture libraries, which proved to be the right direction. Both lauriii and bnjmnm contributed code to the fork, with the final implementation carefully handling browser differences between Chrome/Firefox and Safari's gesture event models. The solution required tuning sensitivity constants based on manual testing to achieve the right feel, demonstrating the value of iterative UX refinement.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
